tasks.name: Create a collection of 3 deep-sea mollusk species longer than 10 cm living below 1000 m
tasks.description: Use advanced search and sorting to find marine mollusk species with a recorded minimum depth of 1000 m or deeper and a shell/body length of at least 10 cm, then create a collection named “Deep Sea Study” that contains exactly 3 such species selected from the top of the results list.
steps: Navigate to the website homepage.
Click on the “Advanced Search” option in the main navigation menu.
In the habitat type filter section, select the checkbox labeled “Marine”.
In the depth range filter, locate the “Minimum depth (m)” field and enter “1000”.
In the size filter section, locate the “Minimum shell/body length (cm)” field and enter “10”.
Click the “Apply filters” or “Search” button to display species matching these criteria.
In the results page, click the sort dropdown and select “Maximum recorded depth – highest first”.
Open the first three species in the sorted results by clicking each of their species names to open their detail pages in new tabs.

On the detail page of the first species, click the “Add to collection” button, choose “Create new collection”, type “Deep Sea Study” as the collection name, and click “Save”; then on the other two species pages, click “Add to collection”, select the existing “Deep Sea Study” collection from the list, and click the button to add each species.

tasks.name: Select the Tridacna species with the highest conservation risk and add it to a comparison list
tasks.description: Find all species within the genus Tridacna, sort them by conservation status severity (from most threatened to least), select the single species with the highest risk category (e.g., CR above EN above VU above LC), and add that species to a comparison list named “High-risk clams”.
steps: From the homepage, click on the main search bar in the header.
Type “Tridacna” into the search field and press Enter to view search results.
In the search results, locate and click the taxon filter or category control, then select “Genus: Tridacna” to restrict results to species in this genus.
On the Tridacna species results list, click the sort dropdown and choose the option that sorts by “Conservation status (most threatened first)”.
Identify the first species in this sorted list (the one with the highest conservation risk category) and click its species name to open the detail page.
On the species detail page, click the “Add to comparison” button.
When prompted, choose “Create new comparison list”, type “High-risk clams” into the list name field, and click the button to create the list.
Confirm the species is added by clicking the “View comparison list” or “High-risk clams” link in the comparison panel to open the comparison page showing this species.

tasks.name: Build an ‘Invasive NA Freshwater’ collection with 2 invasive mollusks recorded after 1950
tasks.description: Use filters to find freshwater mollusk species that are marked as invasive and present in North America, then from those, select two species whose ‘First recorded in region’ year is 1950 or later and save them into a collection named “Invasive NA Freshwater”.
steps: Navigate to the homepage and click on “Advanced Search” or “Species Filter” in the main navigation.
In the habitat filter section, select the checkbox for “Freshwater”.
In the distribution or region filter, select “North America” from the region list.
In the status or impact filter section, select the checkbox labeled “Invasive / Introduced”.
In the additional filters area, locate the field labeled “First record year (≥)” and enter “1950”.
Click the “Apply filters” or “Search” button to list species that match all of these criteria.
From the results, click on the first species name to open its detail page and then click the “Add to collection” button, choose “Create new collection”, name it “Invasive NA Freshwater”, and save.

Return to the filtered results list (using the browser tab or site navigation), click on the second species name in the list to open its detail page, and click “Add to collection”, selecting the existing “Invasive NA Freshwater” collection to add this second species.

tasks.name: Identify the largest Indo-Pacific cephalopod (≥50 cm) and add it to a ‘Giant Cephalopods’ list
tasks.description: Filter cephalopod species from the Indo-Pacific region with a maximum length of at least 50 cm, sort them by maximum length, and then add the single largest species to a list named “Giant Cephalopods”.
steps: From the homepage, click on the “Taxa” or “Browse by Group” menu and select the “Cephalopods” category.
On the cephalopod listing page, open the region filter and select “Indo-Pacific” from the available regions.
Open the size filter panel, locate the “Minimum maximum length (cm)” field, and enter “50”.
Click the “Apply filters” button to show only Indo-Pacific cephalopod species with a maximum length of 50 cm or more.
On the filtered results, click the sort dropdown and choose “Maximum length – largest first”.
Click on the first species in the sorted list (the largest one) to open its species detail page.
On the species detail page, click the “Add to list” or “Add to collection” button.
In the dialog that appears, choose “Create new list”, type “Giant Cephalopods” into the list name field, and click the button to save the species to this list.

tasks.name: Find a cephalopod camouflage teaching resource (ages 12–14, from 2019–2024) and favorite it
tasks.description: Locate an educational resource about cephalopod camouflage intended for ages 12–14 and published between 2019 and 2024, then add that single resource to your favorites list.
steps: From the homepage, click on the “Education” or “Teaching Resources” link in the main navigation.
On the teaching resources page, click the search or keyword field and type “cephalopod camouflage”, then press Enter to search.
In the filters sidebar, locate the “Target age” or “Education level” filter and select the option corresponding to ages “12–14” or “Middle school (12–14)”.
Locate the “Publication year” filter and set the range so the start year is “2019” and the end year is “2024”.
Click the “Apply filters” or “Update results” button to show only resources matching the keyword, age range, and publication years.
From the filtered results list, click on the first resource title to open its detail page.
On the resource detail page, click the “Add to favorites” or star icon button to favorite this resource.
Click on the “Favorites” or “My favorites” link in the header or sidebar to open the favorites page listing this resource.

tasks.name: Compare distributions of a species found in both Japan and Australia with a Japan-only species
tasks.description: Use the distribution map and filters to find one mollusk species that occurs in both Japan and Australia and another species that occurs in Japan but not in Australia, then add both species to a comparison view named “Japan vs Australia”.
steps: From the homepage, click on the “Interactive Map” or “Distribution Map” option in the main navigation.
On the map interface, open the region or country filter and select “Japan” and “Australia” simultaneously so that species present in both countries are highlighted in the results list.
From the list of species associated with both Japan and Australia, click on the first species name to open its detail page in a new tab.
On this species detail page, click the “Add to comparison” button and in the dialog, create a new comparison named “Japan vs Australia”.
Return to the map tab and adjust the region filter to select “Japan” only while explicitly deselecting “Australia”.
From the updated species list (Japan-only), click on the first species name and open its detail page.
On this second species’ detail page, click the “Add to comparison” button and select the existing “Japan vs Australia” comparison from the list to add it.
Click the “View comparison” or “Japan vs Australia” link in the comparison panel to open the comparison page showing both species’ distribution information side by side.

tasks.name: Bookmark the earliest described Muricidae species from the Western Atlantic
tasks.description: Within the family Muricidae, filter species to those occurring in the Western Atlantic, sort them by year of description from oldest to newest, select the earliest described species, and bookmark it.
steps: From the homepage, click on the “Taxa” or “Browse by Family” menu and select the family “Muricidae”.
On the Muricidae family page, open the region filter and select “Western Atlantic” from the list of regions.
Locate the filter or field for “Year described” and leave it empty so that all years in the Western Atlantic are included, then click the “Apply filters” button.
On the filtered results list of Muricidae species, click the sort dropdown and select “Year described – oldest first”.
Click on the first species in this sorted list (the earliest described one) to open its species detail page.
On the species detail page, click the “Bookmark” or bookmark icon button to add the species to your bookmarked items.
Click on the “Bookmarks” or “My bookmarks” link in the header or user tools menu to open the bookmarks page displaying this species.

tasks.name: Create a ‘Venomous snails’ collection with 3 species having a toxoglossan radula
tasks.description: Use the trait or glossary filters to find mollusk species with the radula type trait set to ‘toxoglossan’, then select the first three species from the results and save them into a collection called “Venomous snails”.
steps: Navigate to the homepage and click on the “Traits” or “Morphology” search option in the main navigation.
In the trait filter panel, locate the “Radula type” dropdown or checklist and select the option labeled “Toxoglossan”.
If available, in the taxonomic filter, restrict results to “Gastropoda” to focus on snails, then click the “Apply filters” or “Search” button.
On the resulting species list, click the first species name to open its detail page in a new tab.
On this species detail page, click the “Add to collection” button, choose “Create new collection”, type “Venomous snails” in the collection name field, and click the button to save.
Return to the filtered species list tab and click on the second species name to open its detail page, then click “Add to collection” and select the existing “Venomous snails” collection to add it.
Repeat the process for the third species in the results list by opening its detail page and using the “Add to collection” button to add it to the “Venomous snails” collection.
Click on the “Collections” or “My collections” link in the header or user menu, then select the “Venomous snails” collection to open the page listing the three added species.
